coredata.dc:title: Development of metal enhanced fluorescent nanomaterials featuring gold nanocubes in proximity to carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Goh, Eunseo
Kim, Geun Wan
Ha, Ji Won
Lee, Young Hoon
Lee, Min Hyung
Lee, Hye Jin
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, gold nanocube (AuNC) particles conjugated with a carbon nanodot (CND) layer and spaced with polyethylene glycol (PEG) were prepared to generate a metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect. This is the first report of MEF from gold nanoparticles in proximity to CNDs. Biocompatible materials (AuNCs, CNDs, and PEGs) were used to fabricate the proposed AuNC-CND complex, thus providing potential utilities of fluorescent materials in various nanobiotechnology fields. Additionally, the use of AuNCs played a critical role in controlling the size and shape of amorphous CNDs exhibiting a soft polymer cluster morphology stemming from bottom-up synthesis. We also attempted to explain the complex interplay between the quenching and enhancing processes through the energy transfer direction between the AuNCs and CNDs based on our experimental results for photoluminescence emission spectra, dark-field microscope imaging, and single-particle scattering measurements in relation to differences in the size of core metals, and the concentration of fluorophores.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanocubes
Au nanocube-Carbon dot conjugates
Metal-enhanced fluorescence
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Emission enhancements of CNDs with AuNC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis extinction spectra (black lines) for synthesized CNDs and AuNCs with different x-axis lengths. The maximum peaks are 347, 525, 544, and 570 nm, respectively. The blue line represents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra for the emission of aqueous CNDs with the maximum peak at 447 nm. (B–D) TEM images of synthesized CNDs, ∼17 nm AuNCs, and ∼74 nm AuNCs, respectively.
(A) Rapid degradation of non-PEGylated AuNCs (∼10 min) in acidic conditions (MES buffer, pH 6.0). (B) The stability of PEGylated AuNCs in the same acidic conditions over several months.

(A–C) Representative TEM images of AuNC-CND complexes with different concentrations of CNDs and PEG3000: (A) bare AuNCs and AuNC-CND complexes with (B) 6.5 and (C) 65 mg of CNDs. (D–F) EDS mapping images; panel D in red and E in green represent Au and C, respectively, while panel F displays Au and C together in one location. The average size of the AuNCs shown above is ∼48 nm.

Relative photoluminescence emission intensities under 365 nm excitation for various AuNC-CND complexes. The emission spectra of AuNC-CND complexes (the red and blue lines represent quenching and enhancement, respectively) for different AuNC particle sizes: (A) ∼17, (C) ∼48, and (D) ∼74 nm. 65 mg of CNDs were used for these complexes. (B) presents the emission spectra of ∼48 nm AuNC-CND complexes fabricated with 6.5 mg of CNDs.

(A–B) Single-particle scattering spectra of PEGylated AuNCs (left) and AuNC-CND complexes (right) with an x-axis length of 48 nm and 74 nm, respectively. A single PEGylated AuNC particle strongly absorbs and scatters light at an average wavelength of 553 nm with an x-axis length of 48 nm, and 577 nm with an x-axis length of 74 nm. However, no noticeable peaks were found in the scattering spectra of the AuNC-CND complexes, with plasmon damping probably occurring due to energy transfer from the surface plasmons to the CNDs. (C–D) Representative single-particle dark-field microscope images of PEGylated AuNCs (left) and AuNC-CND complexes (right) with an x-axis length of 48 nm and 74 nm, respectively. The numbers at the bottom right of each panel show the corresponding average scattering intensity/number of measured particles. For PEGylated AuNCs, integration times for the acquisition of scattering images were 10 times lower than those of AuNC-CND complex samples. The average intensity was normalized to the other cases under the assumption that the signals increase linearly with integration time.

A schematic overview of the proposed approach to synthesize AuNC-CND conjugates. (i) CTAB molecules surrounding an AuNC, which is the inevitable outcome of the seed-mediated growth method, were effectively exchanged with HS-PEG-COOHs in a Tween 20 and BSPP environment. (ii) PEGylated AuNCs were then exposed to an aqueous CND solution, resulting in COOH groups at the end of each PEG chain linked to the amine-enriched CNDs via EDC/NHSS cross-linking chemistry.
Reagent dosage in the PEGylation process for the three AuNC sizes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: x represents the MW of PEG varied from 1000, 3000 to 5000.

head.ce:title: Development of metal enhanced fluorescent nanomaterials featuring gold nanocubes in proximity to carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Green-Nano Materials Research Center, Kyungpook National University, 80 Daehakro, Buk-gu, Daegu-city, 41566, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Green-Nano Materials Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Green-Nano Materials Research Center, Kyungpook National University, 80 Daehakro, Buk-gu, Daegu-city, 41566, Republic of Korea.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, gold nanocube (AuNC) particles conjugated with a carbon nanodot (CND) layer and spaced with polyethylene glycol (PEG) were prepared to generate a metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect. This is the first report of MEF from gold nanoparticles in proximity to CNDs. Biocompatible materials (AuNCs, CNDs, and PEGs) were used to fabricate the proposed AuNC-CND complex, thus providing potential utilities of fluorescent materials in various nanobiotechnology fields. Additionally, the use of AuNCs played a critical role in controlling the size and shape of amorphous CNDs exhibiting a soft polymer cluster morphology stemming from bottom-up synthesis. We also attempted to explain the complex interplay between the quenching and enhancing processes through the energy transfer direction between the AuNCs and CNDs based on our experimental results for photoluminescence emission spectra, dark-field microscope imaging, and single-particle scattering measurements in relation to differences in the size of core metals, and the concentration of fluorophores.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is an invaluable chemical phenomenon employed in a wide range of biomedical applications because it is the foundation of many recognition tools used in biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery―i.e., labels, probes, reporters, indicators, or tracers [1,2]. In these biological applications, high photostability, monodispersity, and brightness are essentially required for fluorescent materials. Conventional fluorescent materials such as organic fluorescent dyes [3,4] and quantum dots [5], however, have had certain limitations in that regard. To overcome these limitations, extensive research on core-shell nanoparticles exhibiting metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) has been reported [6]. MEF is a phenomenon where the fluorescence intensity can be increased when the metal is close to fluorophores under specific condition [7]. Nevertheless, challenges such as bleaching, a short shelf-life, high environmental toxicity, and low biocompatibility remain unresolved [8–10]. In this respect, carbon nanodots (CNDs) appear to be promising hybrid MEF nanoparticles because they are non-toxic, biocompatible, eco-friendly, economical, and chemically and optically stable, while also offering a number of facile synthesis methodologies [11–13].

One of the most commonly used metal species for MEF platform with CNDs is Ag [14,15]. For example, Ag@SiO2-CNDs showed four-fold increase of fluorescence intensity [15]. However, the use of Ag particles conjugated with CNDs as a fluorescence tag for in vivo environment is less investigated considering the Ag nanoparticles are known to be toxic to the mammalian cells [16]. On the contrary, Au nanoparticles can be used as one of the most appropriate theranostic probes due to their bioinertness as well as physical and multifunctional properties [17]. There have been few works on synthesizing Au-CNDs hybrids and understanding their optical properties [18,19]; Ni et al. [19] have developed Au nanorod-CNDs hybrids as a probe for theranostic applications while taking the risk of quenching effect of gold. Li et al. [18] have also synthesized Au-CND hybrids to investigate the quenching dependency on the size of Au particles and CND concentration. Instead of using CNDs, Hong et al. [20] have studied the MEF of single-wall carbon nanotubes on a gold substrate. Nevertheless, due to the high fluorescence quenching effect of gold [21], it is still challenging to establish robust experimental conditions for MEF when using Au nanoparticles such as controlling shape of particles, the distance between Au and fluorophores, and concentration of fluorophores [22]. In this article, we demonstrate for the first time gold nanocube (AuNC) particles decorated with a CND layer in conjunction with PEG spacers with the goal of generating an MEF effect (Scheme 1 ). Our combined AuNC-CND complex in this work exhibited the potential for use as a uniformly sized and shaped fluorescent tag. This is essential when utilizing CNDs as an emitter because CND clusters synthesized using a bottom-up approach typically exhibit different amorphous cluster morphologies. An approximate 2.2-fold increase in fluorescence intensity was achieved using AuNCs with a specific size (48 nm) and shape, an AuNC:CND atomic ratio of roughly 1:0.04, and PEG spacers with a length of 10 nm.

Gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4⋅3H2O, Sigma-Aldrich), hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, Tokyo Chemical Industry), hexadecyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC, Tokyo Chemical Industry), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, Sigma-Aldrich), l-ascorbic acid (Sigma-Aldrich), citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich), 50% (w/w) ethylenediamine (Sigma-Aldrich), sodium bromide (NaBr, Sigma-Aldrich), Tween 20 (Sigma-Aldrich), bis(p-sulfonatophenyl)phenylphosphine dihydrate dipotassium salt (BSPP, Sigma-Aldrich), o-(3-carboxypropyl)-o′-[2-(3-mercaptopropionylamino)ethyl]-polyethylene glycol (HS-PEG1,000∼5,000-COOH, average molecular weight ∼1–5 kDa, Sigma-Aldrich), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES, C6H13NO4S, Sigma-Aldrich), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, Thermo), sulfo-N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHSS, Thermo), 2-mercaptoethanol (Sigma-Aldrich), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich) were all used as received. Millipore-filtered water was used to prepare all of the aqueous solutions.

CND was synthesized using the method developed by Zhu et al. [23]. Briefly, 10 mL of Millipore-filtered water was mixed with 1.051 g of citric acid and 335 μL of 50% (w/w) ethylenediamine. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave reactor and heated at 150 °C for 5 h 20 min. After the reaction, the liquid product was converted into powder form via vacuum distillation or freeze drying.

Depending on the average AuNC size required, three previously reported seed growth methods [24–26] were modified and employed. The optical properties of the AuNC colloidal solutions and the size of the particles were characterized using a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT 7700).

The seeding and growth synthesis method described by Park et al. [24] was used to synthesize AuNCs with an average x-axis length of about 17 nm. First, a 1–2nm seed solution was prepared, in which 9.75 mL of 0.1 M CTAB was mixed with 250 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4 followed by the addition of 600 μL of 0.01 M NaBH4 and thorough stirring for 3 min. The solution was then kept at 27 °C for 3 h prior to use. Next, a 10 nm seed solution was synthesized by successively adding 2 mL of 0.2 M CTAC, 1.5 mL of 0.1 M l-ascorbic acid, 50 μL of the 1–2 nm seed solution, and 2 mL of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 to a separate vial. The mixed solution was stirred continuously at 300 rpm for 15 min and then centrifuged twice (20,600 g, 30 min) using Millipore-filtered water. The particles were then redispersed in 1 mL of 20 mM CTAC solution. Finally, a growth solution was prepared by sequentially adding 6 mL of 0.1 M CTAC, 30 μL of 120 mM NaBr, 300 μL of the 10 nm seed solution, 390 μL of 10 mM l-ascorbic acid, and 6 mL of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 solution to a separate vial. The mixed solution was continuously stirred at 500 rpm for 25 min.

The seed growth method previously described by Wu et al. [26] was modified [27,28] and used to synthesize AuNCs with an average length of ∼48 nm. A seed solution was prepared by sequentially mixing 7.5 mL of 0.1 M CTAB with 250 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4 and 600 μL of 0.01 M NaBH4, followed by thorough stirring for 2 min. The solution was then kept at 29 °C for 1 h prior to use. A growth solution was prepared by successively adding 6.4 mL of 0.01 M CTAB, 32 mL of Millipore-filtered water, 800 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4, 3.8 mL of 0.1 M l-ascorbic acid, and 20 μL of the 10-times-diluted seed solution, followed by mixing until the solution had turned light crimson. The solution was then kept undisturbed at 29 °C overnight.

The seed growth method developed by Wu et al. [25] was used to synthesize AuNC particles with an average length of ∼74 nm. A seed solution was prepared by dissolving 0.32 g of CTAC in 5 mL of Millipore-filtered water followed by mixing with 5 mL of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 solution. Next, 450 μL of 0.02 M NaBH4 solution was added to the mixed solution followed by thorough stirring for 2 min. The solution was aged at 30 °C for 1 h prior to use. Separately, two growth solutions were prepared by sequentially adding 9.625 mL of 0.1 M CTAC, 250 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4 solution, 10 μL of 0.01 M NaBr solution, and 90 μL of 0.04 M l-ascorbic acid to two separate vials under continuous stirring at 700 rpm. To only one of these vials was 25 μL of the seed solution added under stirring until the solution turned light pink. Finally, 45 μL of this solution was injected into the second vial under thorough stirring until the solution turned light purple (∼20 s). The solution was then kept undisturbed for 15 min.

Prior to the PEGylation process, the excess CTAB or CTAC in our synthesized AuNC solution was removed using centrifugation (6000 g, 20 min) several times and the NC particles were resuspended in 1 mM CTAB or CTAC solution. The following PEGylation process was modified from a method originally developed for gold nanorods [29]. First, 40 mL of AuNC solution with an optical density of 1.16 was concentrated 10 times via centrifugation (6000 g, 20 min), followed by re-dispersing the NCs in 4 mL of Millipore-filtered water. Next, 4 mL of the concentrated AuNC solutions for each AuNC size were mixed successively with different volumes of 2 vol% Tween 20, 0.1 M BSPP, 1.6 mM HS-PEGX-COOH (x is the molecular weight (MW) of PEG ranging from 1000, 3000 to 5000), and Millipore-filtered water (Table 1 ). Each of these solutions was continuously stirred at 900 rpm for 24 h. Centrifugation of the solutions was repeated several times (6000 g, 30 min) with Millipore-filtered water and MES buffer (0.05 M, pH 6.0). The PEGylated NCs particles were finally re-dispersed in 8 mL of the MES buffer.

First, 2 mL of concentrated PEGylated AuNC solution was mixed with 8 mL of 0.05 M MES buffer (pH 6.0), Following this, 200 μL of 130 mM EDC and 250 mM sulfo-NHS freshly prepared in an MES buffer were injected quickly into the AuNC solution under strong, continuous sonication for 15 min. Next, 14 μL of 14.3 M (99%) 2-mercaptoethanol was then introduced to the solution to quench the reaction by forming a stable complex with the remaining carbodiimide [30]. After 10 min, 65 mg or 6.5 mg of CND was added to the PEGylated AuNC solution and the solution pH was adjusted to 7 by adding an appropriate amount of 5 M NaOH solution and allowing the reaction to proceed for 2 h.

The MEF effect when AuNCs were in proximity to CNDs was investigated by varying the AuNC size, and CND concentration. AuNCs with an average x-axis length of ∼17, ∼48, and ∼74 nm were used, while CND concentrations of 6.5 or 65 mg per 10 mL were utilized, primarily to maintain a constant level of fluorescence. In addition, PEG spacers with heterobifunctional groups of both HS and COOH. As seen in Scheme 1, the AuNCs were stabilized with CTAB or CTAC and then PEGylated via the self-assembly of thiol-modified PEGs onto the NC surface in a Tween 20 and BSPP environment, which assists the ligand exchange reaction between PEG and CTAB (or CTAC) without deforming the particles during the reaction [29]. The carboxylic acid terminated PEGylated AuNCs were then covalently linked with amine-enriched CNDs via EDC/NHSS cross-linking chemistry. Amine-enriched CNDs were used for two reasons: (i) CNDs exhibit a high quantum yield (QY) of ∼80% and (ii) they offer easy accessibility when using the EDC/NHSS reaction with a carboxylic group without the need for any further passivation or functionalization [23]. The successful conjugation between the CNDs and AuNCs was confirmed by the appearance of a new energy level in the UV–vis absorption data (Figs. S1–3).

The surface structure and morphology of our AuNC-CND conjugate were validated using TEM and energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping analysis (Figs. 2 and 3, Figs. S4–5, and Table S1). When 65 mg of CNDs was used to synthesize the AuNC-CND complex, a thicker layer surrounding each AuNC particle was observed (Fig. 3B–C). The NC layer was confirmed by the presence of Au, shown in red in Fig. 3D, while the C from the conjugates is shown in Fig. 3E in green. In addition, Au and C together were observed in a single location with an Au core surrounded by C (Fig. 3F), which indicated the successful formation of the AuNC-CND complex (See Fig. S4 in Supplementary Information for a before-and-after comparison of CNDs conjugated onto AuNCs that were only PEGylated).

Additional information on the distance between the AuNC and CNDs was obtained from EDS mapping analysis. The thickness of the green layer in Fig. 3E was ∼10 nm when the AuNC-CND conjugates were synthesized using PEG3000 (i.e., PEG spacers with an average MW of 3000). Given that PEG3000 has a mean number (n = 66) of repeating units in its long polymer chain, the distance between the AuNC and CNDs can theoretically be estimated to be ∼18 nm, which is somewhat longer than the experimental results. This may be because polymers are usually non-linear, with the conformation of PEG spacers varying depending on their density on the NC particles as well as n [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission enhancements of CNDs with AuNC
ce:section.ce:para: The MEF effect originating from the coupling of CNDs to AuNC particles was investigated by monitoring changes in CND fluorescence intensities before and after the formation of the AuNC-CND conjugates. In addition, the effect of AuNC size, CND concentration, and length of PEG spacers on MEF was measured.

Fig. 4A, C, and D show the PL emission spectra for 65 mg of CNDs in 10 mL of Millipore-filtered water before (control samples; the black line) and after (the red and blue lines) conjugation with AuNCs with an average x-axis size of 17, 48, and 74 nm in conjunction with a fixed PEG spacer size of 10 nm (PEG3000). A 2.2 and 2.0-fold increase in the PL emission of CNDs linked to AuNCs with sizes of ∼48 (Fig. 4C) and ∼74 nm (Fig. 4D), respectively, were observed when compared to CNDs only. Our lifetime decay data supports that these enhancements come from the plasmon coupling effect, which is one of the proposed mechanisms to explain MEF phenomena (Fig. S8) [7]. However, the fluorescence intensity dropped to ∼10% for the smallest AuNC size (17 nm) compared to the CNDs (Fig. 4A).

Because severe quenching was observed when the CNDs were too concentrated (Figs. S6–7), we also investigated the effect of CND concentration on the MEF system. When diluted aqueous CNDs (6.5 mg) were used to fabricate the AuNC-CND complex, the fluorescence intensity of the complex fell by 20% (Fig. 4B), compared to the corresponding control solution (6.5 mg of CNDs in Millipore-filtered water only).

The distance-dependence on MEF was analyzed by changing the length of spacer, PEG, between AuNC and CNDs (see Fig. S9). The enhancement effect will rarely appear when the metal is far away from CNDs, whereas such an effect will increase as the distance between the metal and fluorophores gets closer, originated from the increased rate of radiative decay (Γr) or excitation (Em). In very close proximity, quenching will occur dominantly through the resonance energy transfer process resulting in changes of nonradiative decay rate (Γnr) [7]. To determine the optimal distance between AuNC and CNDs, we used three different MW of PEGs as spacers: 1000 (∼4 nm), 3000 (∼10 nm), and 5000 (∼20 nm). As pointed out in section 3.2., these distances were considered experimentally rather than theoretically given that the actual distances of long polymers are highly dependent on their structural formations on particles. Under the same conditions of AuNC size and CND concentration, the fluorescence intensity for the PEG1000 was even lower than that of CNDs only. However, both PEG3000 and PEG5000 showed enhanced brightness compared to the bare CNDs, and the intensity of PEG5000 was slightly higher than that of PEG3000. It is rather difficult to define the ‘quenching distance’, where the resonance energy transfer between AuNC-CND rarely occurs, because unlike the film or substrate [20], the length of spacers on particle also determines the density of fluorophores above the particle surface, which leads to the different level of self-quenching of CNDs [32]. However, this result suggests that over ∼10 nm distance between AuNC-CND is where the enhancement effect exceeds the quenching and self-quenching effect of CNDs.

A distinct peak is typically observed in the scattering spectrum of a single AuNC particle, similar to the absorption spectra of bulk or ensemble solutions (Fig. 1), because of their strong plasmonic properties [24]. As seen in Fig. 5A–B left, single ∼48 nm and ∼74 nm PEGylated AuNC particles strongly scattered the light at around 553 and 577 nm, respectively. However, no noticeable peaks were observed in each scattering spectra of the AuNC-CND complexes regardless of particle size (Fig. 5A–B right). This plasmon damping suggests that there are certain energy transfer from the surface plasmons of the metal to the CNDs. Furthermore, the average scattering intensity of AuNC-CND complex with 6.5 mg CNDs was higher than that of one with 65 mg CNDs (Fig. S10). Given that the latter had more CNDs than the former, it can be inferred that the more CNDs a complex has, the more energy transfer that occurs, thus further supporting the role of CNDs as an energy acceptor in MEF complexes.

In conclusion, we report the generation of MEF from AuNC-CNDs complexes. In order to more clearly elucidate the mechanisms underlying the interaction between the Au particles and CNDs, additional research (e.g., the MEF effect of CNDs on Au substrate) is underway. Nevertheless, because the generation of MEF from AuNCs and CNDs has not previously been reported, our results may open up a new route for understanding the interaction between CNDs and the surface of gold from an MEF perspective. In addition, the use of biocompatible materials (i.e., AuNCs, CNDs, and PEG) to create the MEF complex offers a unique opportunity for the utilization of AuNCs and CNDs in nanobiotechnology applications, including bioimaging and photodynamic therapy.

Eunseo Goh: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Reviewing. Geun Wan Kim: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ji Won Ha: Methodology, Validation, Writing-Reviewing. Young Hoon Lee: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing-Reviewing. Min Hyung Lee: Methodology, Validation, Writing-Reviewing. Hye Jin Lee: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, ∗All authors read and approved the final manuscript prior to submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (Ministry of Science and ICT, MSIT) (grant number: NRF-2020R1A4A1018393).
